summary: Test whether each individual graded synapse in the pyloric network is dispensable by sweeping each synaptic conductance independently toward zero (and beyond) across several orders of magnitude, plus a random-directions probe in synaptic-only space to catch joint fragility not visible in single-parameter sweeps.

interpretation.evidence: The hypothesis claims each individual graded synapse is dispensable (i.e., can be swept to zero without disrupting the rhythm). The data falsify this for at least three synapses: syn3:abpd->py has slopeNearZero=11.416 and toleratedRadius=0.4 (extremely stiff, rhythm breaks very close to nominal conductance), syn6:py->lp has slopeNearZero=2.06 and toleratedRadius=1 with maxDistance=15 (rhythm collapses), and syn4:lp->abpd has slopeNearZero=1.476 and toleratedRadius=1 with maxDistance=15 (rhythm collapses). These synapses are clearly not dispensable. By contrast, syn2:abpd->py shows slopeNearZero=0.009 and maxDistance=0.096, suggesting it is nearly dispensable. The random-directions probe with meanDistanceAligned=13.719 and fractionAligned=0.015 shows that almost any joint perturbation in synaptic space at radius=3 also destroys the rhythm, confirming broad fragility rather than robustness. The hypothesis of universal dispensability is falsified by the high-slope, low-toleratedRadius synapses.
interpretation.refinedClaim: The pyloric rhythm is critically dependent on at least syn3:abpd->py (toleratedRadius≈0.4, slope≈11.4), syn4:lp->abpd, and syn6:py->lp (both with maxDistance=15 at radius=1), making these synapses indispensable; only syn2:abpd->py (slope≈0.009, maxDistance≈0.096) approaches true dispensability. A sharper falsifiable claim: removing any one of {syn3, syn4, syn6} individually will drive phase-shape distance above 1.0, while removing syn2 alone will not exceed 0.1.
